We use the first shifting theorem whenever we are taking Laplace transforms and there is a rogue exponential function in the t variable OR we are taking inverse Laplace transforms and there is a shift in the s variable.

Example 5 Consider the function

f (t) = 6tu(t ‚ąí 3) + 9tu(t ‚ąí 7) ‚ąí 15u(t ‚ąí 12).

a) Write the function without the use of Heavisides.

b) Sketch the function.

c) Find the Laplace transform of f .

L(f (t ‚ąí c)u(t ‚ąí c)) = e‚ąícs F (s)

We use the second shifting theorem whenever we are taking inverse Laplace transforms and there is a rogue exponential function in the s variable OR we are taking Laplace transforms and there is a shift and a cut in the t variable. If we have a cut in the t variable without a shift we need to manipulate the function so that a shift appears.

Example 9 (Challenge Problem) Ô£Ī Ô£≤ 1, 0 < t ‚Č§ 1; Suppose that f (t) = ‚ąí1, 1 < t ‚Č§ 2; f (t ‚ąí 2), otherwise. Ô£≥

Sketch the function and find its Laplace transform.

Note that the condition f (t) = f (t ‚ąí 2) simply forces the function to repeat every 2 units. That is, it forces a periodicity of 2.

[Hint: You may need the theory of limiting sums of geometric series.]
